opus_reasoning: The Besht teaches his son a divine Name and instructs him to meditate on it (תכוון שם זה), an explicit kabbalistic kavvanah; also references sod ha-ibbur.
Besht took his pipe (לקח הלולקע).
The story explicitly depicts multiple revelations: dream-revelations telling Eliezer not to flee, heavenly disclosures of war strategy, and Elijah appearing to announce the birth of the Besht.
The tzaddik is a famous, publicly renowned rabbi/advisor to the Kaiser, not a hidden righteous person.
The story explicitly takes place during Sukkot (חה״מ סוכות) when the tefillin incident occurs.
The rebbe reveals hidden heavenly secrets (the man's past deed, the heavenly court's decree) to his followers — a direct mystical disclosure (giluy).
R. Nachman perceives and converses with the deceased Baal Shem Tov at his grave, who instructs him to travel to the Land of Israel — a clear supernatural perception others present could not see.
The Baal Shem Tov mystically perceives a light/aura resting on the pious woman Rivele and supernaturally hears the heavenly accusation against the sick doctor — direct mystical disclosures.
The boy belatedly realizes the supposed in-laws are actually idolatrous priests who lured him as a sacrifice — a clear retrospective recognition of hidden identity/situation.
The story centers on repentance/teshuvah and the power of storytelling, not on a general virtue being illustrated.
Mentions 'צרות של ישראל' (troubles of Israel/Jewish people) but not the Land of Israel as setting or destination; no depiction of Eretz Yisrael.
Story centers on a dream-instructed preaching mission and the Besht protecting him from the Samael; no prediction by a tzaddik that comes true drives the arc.
The Besht performs 'שמות הקדושים' (yihudim with divine names) contemplatively to extract a hidden sod from Samael.

The Besht uses mystical means (immersion in river, then mere appearance at the window) to free the man's son and frighten the lord — accusation of kishuf and concrete supernatural effect fit practical kabbalah.
Mention of seeking a river/water to immerse for Shabbat, but no mikveh scene actually occurs.
Story about R. Shneur Zalman of Liadi's arrest; no hidden righteous/lamed-vavnik figure depicted.

Story involves hidden mystical power in a mezuzah inscribed with R. Naftali's name, angelic names/supernal lights speculation, and a dream-revelation — esoteric/kabbalistic knowledge about name-power.
Story involves Besht's miraculous powers, oaths altering nature, and Elijah's intervention, but does not depict kabbalistic/esoteric doctrine or hidden mystical knowledge being taught or transmitted.
The story mentions רעש ורוגז (anger/uproar) from the father, not physical trembling as religious/supernatural response.

The mysterious old innkeeper who joyfully serves all wayfarers, lives ascetically on bread and water, and whose blessing-fulfilled son the narrator encounters reads as a hidden righteous figure (tzaddik nistar) embodying hachnasat orchim.
No family members of the tzaddik appear; the story involves the tzaddik (R. Leib Sarah's) and an avrekh/bride, not the tzaddik's family.
Story centers on a hidden tzaddik causing the Besht to desecrate Shabbat as atonement, not on a fulfilled prediction/prophecy.
Story depicts demonic harassment and rabbinic intervention/protection, not a mystical revelation (giluy) to the protagonist.
The climactic storytelling failure and recovery occurs across the three Shabbat meals at the gvir's table, with Shabbat centrally framing the events.

The deceased is shown post-mortem being judged, and his self-mortification/tenitot are credited as atoning for his sin of inattention in prayer, framing fasting-as-teshuvah; the community also reforms its conduct in response.
Discrete supernatural events: Besht's amulets lose then regain efficacy, and R. Yitzhak miraculously discovers it is Friday.
The woman belatedly learns the stranger is not her uncle but the Besht, and the disciples/father-in-law come to recognize the hidden truth about her situation.
The story features supernatural travel/hidden tzaddikim and a wandering dead soul, but no discrete miracle event with clear before/after benefit to a recipient.
The Besht protects himself from the officer's sword by transferring the paralysis to the attacker, simultaneously healing the sick woman—central act of protection.
Story mentions kavvanot in Shema prayer but no Lurianic yihudim (unifications of divine names) are depicted.
No belated recognition; the story shows Besht's foreknowledge fulfilled, not a character realizing a hidden truth after the fact.

The story depicts the Besht's mystical visions on Shabbat, soul-binding (התקשרות נפש ברוח ונשמה), and identification of a heretical Sabbatean book through hidden knowledge — clearly esoteric/mystical content.
Story concerns a fraudulent get and the tzaddik's ruach hakodesh revealing it; no kabbalistic/esoteric doctrine depicted.
The Besht predicts that R. Abba will forget ya'aleh ve-yavo a second time, and the prediction is confirmed as true.
The Baal Shem Tov gives prophetic advice and the story involves a hidden tzaddik (Yonah ben Amitai), but there is no use of amulets, names, or practical kabbalistic techniques depicted.
The story explicitly depicts the Baal Shem Tov 'מיחד יחודים' (performing yichudim, Lurianic kavvanot) with face burning like torches.
No maggid/preacher character appears in the story.
Story explicitly describes kavvanah on Shema Yisrael with a hindrance (ש״ו standing opposite) and the Besht instructing him to perform this kavvanah — Lurianic mystical intention in prayer.
No death is depicted; the story involves a Shabbat ordeal and revelation about a former servant, with no death event.
The wife of the avrekh dies ('מתה עליו אשתו'), a depicted death event.
'מזמור שיר' psalm title, not depicted music.
The Besht reveals supernaturally that Samael tried to seize the maggid, and the rabbi of Lemberg receives a dream-vision of the Besht instructing him — direct mystical disclosures.
The rich man cries out in fear with his hair standing on end ('סמרו שערות בשרו פלצות אחזוהו') upon hearing the tale.
No dream is depicted; the story involves visits to a cave, imprisonment, and miraculous rescue, but no dream sequence.
The Baal Shem Tov uses the Zohar to see his location—a holy/mystical practice, not forbidden magic, witchcraft, or non-Jewish sorcery.
R. Gershon of Kitov, the Baal Shem Tov's brother-in-law (gis), is a central character, and the story opens with him traveling to arrange marriages for his sons—immediate family of the tsaddik.
The Maggid tests the Besht and is humbled, but the story frames it as a learning encounter rather than explicitly depicting pride as a moral failing.
The Baal Shem Tov predicts the birth of a son and later the son's transformation, both of which come true as foretold.
The story describes the Besht's death, prophetic visions, and soul ascent instructions, but does not depict a character experiencing mystical cleaving/communion with God (devekut).
Besht sitting and performing yichudim (מיחד יחודים).
The Besht's prayer raises souls of the dead (mechayeh metim kavvanot), and when R' Gershon attempts the same kavvanah, dead souls appear to him as a great flock — discrete supernatural events.
Though a bride and groom seek a blessing before their wedding, the story centers on the tzadik's miraculous rescue and confession, not on marriage as event or institution.
The Besht goes to the mikveh before prayer as part of the scene.
No Jewish holiday is depicted; mentions only Shabbat and weekday events.
The Besht predicts that Reb Abba will forget ya'aleh ve-yavo again even if he repeats the prayer, which then comes true.
No depiction of heaven/upper worlds as setting; mentions a 'sar haTorah/Esh' being brought down but action stays on earth.
The Baal Shem Tov supernaturally reveals to the scholar that he is the reincarnation of a wicked man who died sixty years prior — a direct mystical disclosure.
The Maggid of Mezeritch is explicitly referenced as a distinct character (הרב המגיד הגדול ממעזריטש) whose teachings Shneur Zalman traveled to hear.
The Besht prays in the beit midrash (בבהמ"ד) in the story.
The avrekh already sought closeness to the rebbe from the start; he is not a skeptic/outsider being drawn near—the story is about the rebbe arranging his marriage/fortune.
The Rebbe's prescient knowledge is attributed to divine wisdom/ruach hakodesh, not practical kabbalah techniques like amulets, names, or spirit manipulation.
The rabbi's teaching explicitly frames the guest's duty as doing teshuvah to repair his sins and ease the host's burden, making repentance a central theme.
The story takes place on Rosh Hashanah (ר״ה), not Shabbat.
Story mentions kavanah in Shema but no Lurianic yihudim are depicted.
Story centers on a dispute between tzaddikim; mentions 'מלשינים' (informers/slanderers) only in passing, with no concrete depiction of a character lying or deceiving.
The story mentions praying 'with kavanah' generally but does not invoke Lurianic kavvanot as a mystical technical practice.
Story involves visits to rebbes but does not depict the court space itself.
Story involves demons/klipot and a tzaddik's protective formula, but no actual kabbalistic/esoteric doctrine or hidden mystical knowledge is taught or revealed.
The story is a miraculous-rescue/divine-providence tale (mitzvah of brit milah delaying the tzaddik saves his life), not focused on illustrating a specific virtue of the tzaddik.
The Besht promises the childless couple a son, but no birth is actually depicted in the story.
Story involves soul-ascent to heavenly chambers but no Lurianic yihudim (unifications of divine names) are depicted.
The Besht explicitly appeals to 'my rebbe' (author of Toldot Yaakov Yosef) for help opening the gate, depicting a master-disciple relationship central to the story's climax.
The story centers on the revelation of a hidden tzaddik (the seemingly boorish innkeeper) to the Besht and his disciples.
The nobleman's drunken boasting/self-aggrandizement about his inn and his subsequent rage leads to divine punishment (withered hand), depicting pride as a moral failing.
Embedding-only neighbor; no amulet depicted.
Traveled to gather money for ransoming captives (לקבץ ממון על פדיון שבוים).
The Baal Shem Tov goes to the mikveh on erev Shabbat as part of the scene.
The Besht explicitly performs kabbalistic actions 'בשמות הקדושים' to retrieve a divine name/sod from Samael, reflecting Lurianic-style mystical intentions/practices.
The story depicts sin, punishment, suffering, and repentance; no depiction of joy (שמחה) as a religious state.
Parallel of Khal-Hasidim_0082; went to mikveh.
The story depicts the Mokhiach leading prayer on Shabbat and the Dayan cursing him during the Shabbat prayer.
Story involves emperor, antisemitic noble, and robbers, but no mitnagdim or maskilim/non-Hasidic Jewish opponents.
The rabbi reveals a past sin via a dead witness; no prediction of a future event.
Story explicitly reveals hidden heavenly court decrees, mystical power of mitzvot, and a secret reason for the man's drunkenness — esoteric knowledge revealed by the tzaddik.

R' Chaim is depicted as a hidden righteous man who concealed himself as a baker's son-in-law for two years, studying secretly with Elijah the Prophet who reveals he appears to such hidden humble tzaddikim.
Story involves the Besht, a rabbi, and a scribe dealing with demons; no master-disciple relationship is depicted.
Story takes place in earthly settings (towns, forest, inn); no scenes in heaven or upper worlds.
The story centrally involves the Besht's pledge of 200 zehuvim dowry for orphans and the redemption of captives (pidyon shvuyim) funds, both forms of tzedakah.
The young avrekh is sent by his rebbe as a hidden agent whose Torah words secretly penetrate the innkeeper's heart, functioning as a concealed righteous emissary.
The tzaddik of Shpole swears the Hasid will have no children, and R. Pinchas of Koretz swears he will—both prophecies' fulfillment (and the descendant's betrayal) drive the narrative arc.
Story concerns a hidden-tzaddik tailor and rabbinic succession in Lublin; no skeptic/outsider is drawn closer to a tzaddik or Hasidism through the narrative.
No immediate family members of a tsaddik appear; characters are the tsaddik and his friend/colleague.
Parallel; decided not to go to the mikveh.
The Besht uses mystical power to revive a dead bride from her grave and adjudicates between her and a dead soul — a concrete miraculous healing/spirit manipulation.
Story describes Besht's revelation, asceticism, and healing but does not mention Lurianic kavvanot or kabbalistic intentions in prayer.
The story depicts the Besht in conflict with Samael (the demonic adversary), diverting prayers away from him.

The story mentions sounds, lightnings and torches during mystical Torah revelation, but this is a theophany/revelation scene rather than light/fire surrounding a person as a sign of his righteousness.
'ימחול' = please/forgive.
Story concerns the Besht's hidden righteousness, hospitality, prayer, and revelation; no depiction of almsgiving or charity to the poor.
No dream is depicted in the story.
